title: Parametric curve arc length
description: Use definite integrals to find the length of a curve defined by a parametric equation.
topic_path: Parametric equations, polar coordinates, and vector-valued functions
Finding arc lengths of curves given by parametric equations

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the parametric curve:

$\begin{align}
x&=5t^2
\\\\
y&=2e^t
\end{align}$

**Which integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=-1$ to $t=3$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's find $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[5t^2\right]
\\\\
&=10t
\\\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[2e^t\right]
\\\\
&=2e^t
\end{align}$

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{-1}^3 \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{-1}^3 \sqrt{(10t)^2+(2e^t)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{-1}^3 \sqrt{100t^2+4e^{2t}}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=-1$ to $t=3$:

$\displaystyle\int_{-1}^3 \sqrt{100t^2+4e^{2t}}\,dt$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the parametric curve:

$\begin{align}
x&=\cos(2t)
\\\\
y&=6t^{3}
\end{align}$

**Which integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=a$ to $t=b$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's find $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\cos(2t)\right]
\\\\
&=-\sin(2t)(2)
\\\\
&=-2\sin(2t)
\\\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[6t^{3}\right]
\\\\
&=18t^2
\end{align}$

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{\left(-2\sin(2t)\right)^2+\left(18t^2\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{4\sin^2(2t)+324t^4}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=a$ to $t=b$:

$\displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{4\sin^2(2t)+324t^4}\,dt$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the parametric curve:

$\begin{align}
x&=2t^{-2}
\\\\
y&=\ln(t^3)
\end{align}$

**Which integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=2$ to $t=7$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{2}^{7} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{2}^{7} \sqrt{\left(-\dfrac{4}{t^3}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{3}{t}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{2}^{7} \sqrt{\dfrac{16}{t^{6}}+\dfrac{9}{t^2}}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=2$ to $t=7$:

$\displaystyle\int_{2}^{7} \sqrt{\dfrac{16}{t^{6}}+\dfrac{9}{t^2}}\,dt$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the parametric curve:

$\begin{align}
x&=7e^t
\\\\
y&=3t^{5}
\end{align}$

**Which integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=1$ to $t=5$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's find $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[7e^t\right]
\\\\
&=7e^t
\\\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[3t^{5}\right]
\\\\
&=15t^4
\end{align}$

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{1}^{5} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{1}^{5} \sqrt{\left(7e^t\right)^2+\left(15t^4\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{1}^{5} \sqrt{49e^{2t}+225t^8}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=1$ to $t=5$:

$\displaystyle\int_{1}^{5} \sqrt{49e^{2t}+225t^8}\,dt$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the parametric curve:

$\begin{align}
x&=t^{2}
\\\\
y&=-4\tan(t)
\end{align}$

**Which integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=-\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ to $t=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's find $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[t^{2}\right]
\\\\
&=2t
\\\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[-4\tan(t)\right]
\\\\
&=-4\sec^2(t)
\end{align}$

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{-\tfrac{\pi}{6}}^{\tfrac{\pi}{3}} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{-\tfrac{\pi}{6}}^{\tfrac{\pi}{3}} \sqrt{\left(2t\right)^2+\left(-4\sec^2(t)\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{-\tfrac{\pi}{6}}^{\tfrac{\pi}{3}} \sqrt{4t^2+16\sec^4(t)}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=-\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ to $t=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$:

$\displaystyle\int_{-\tfrac{\pi}{6}}^{\tfrac{\pi}{3}} \sqrt{4t^2+16\sec^4(t)}\,dt$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the parametric curve:

$\begin{align}
x&=\ln(8t)
\\\\
y&=5t^{3}
\end{align}$

**Which integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=11$ to $t=17$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's find $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\ln(8t)\right]
\\\\
&=\dfrac{8}{8t}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{t}
\\\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[5t^{3}\right]
\\\\
&=15t^2
\end{align}$

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{11}^{17} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{11}^{17} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1}{t}\right)^2+\left(15t^2\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{11}^{17} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{t^2}+225t^4}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=11$ to $t=17$:

$\displaystyle\int_{11}^{17} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{t^2}+225t^4}\,dt$

This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{-5}^{0} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{-5}^{0} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{10}{t^3}\right)^2+\left(-e^{-t}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{-5}^{0} \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{t^6}+e^{-2t}}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=-5$ to $t=0$:

$\displaystyle\int_{-5}^{0} \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{t^6}+e^{-2t}}\,dt$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the parametric curve:

$\begin{align}
x&=\sin^2(t)
\\\\
y&=-9t
\end{align}$

**Which integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=0$ to $t=4$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's find $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\sin^2(t)\right]
\\\\
&=2\sin(t)\cos(t)
\\\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[-9t\right]
\\\\
&=-9
\end{align}$

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{4} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{4} \sqrt{\left(2\sin(t)\cos(t)\right)^2+\left(-9\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{4} \sqrt{4\sin^2(t)\cos^2(t)+81}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=0$ to $t=4$:

$\displaystyle\int_{0}^{4} \sqrt{4\sin^2(t)\cos^2(t)+81}\,dt$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the parametric curve:

$\begin{align}
x&=\ln(t+5)
\\\\
y&=-6t^{5}
\end{align}$

**Which integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=-2$ to $t=3$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's find $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\ln(t+5)\right]
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{t+5}
\\\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[-6t^{5}\right]
\\\\
&=-30t^4
\end{align}$

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{-2}^{3} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{-2}^{3} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1}{t+5}\right)^2+\left(-30t^4\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{-2}^{3} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(t+5)^2}+900t^{8}}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=-2$ to $t=3$:

$\displaystyle\int_{-2}^{3} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(t+5)^2}+900t^{8}}\,dt$

This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's find $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[e^{10t}\right]
\\\\
&=10e^{10t}
\\\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[t^{3}\right]
\\\\
&=3t^2
\end{align}$

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{\left(10e^{10t}\right)^2+\left(3t^2\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{100e^{20t}+9t^4}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=a$ to $t=b$:

$\displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{100e^{20t}+9t^4}\,dt$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the parametric curve:

$\begin{align}
x&=-3t^{4}
\\\\
y&=\cos^3(t)
\end{align}$

**Which integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=8$ to $t=13$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's find $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dt}$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dx}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[-3t^{4}\right]
\\\\
&=-12t^3
\\\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dt}&=\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\cos^3(t)\right]
\\\\
&=3\cos^2(t)(-\sin(t))
\\\\
&=-3\cos^2(t)\sin(t)
\end{align}$

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{8}^{13} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{8}^{13} \sqrt{\left(-12t^3\right)^2+\left(-3\cos^2(t)\sin(t)\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{8}^{13} \sqrt{144t^6+9\cos^4(t)\sin^2(t)}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=8$ to $t=13$:

$\displaystyle\int_{8}^{13} \sqrt{144t^6+9\cos^4(t)\sin^2(t)}\,dt$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the parametric curve:

$\begin{align}
x&=\dfrac{4}{t}
\\\\
y&=\ln(-t)
\end{align}$

**Which integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=-12$ to $t=-5$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is the formula for the arc length of a parametric curve over the interval $[a, b]$:

$\displaystyle\int_a^b\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\, dt$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now we can find the expression for the arc length:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int_{-12}^{-5} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{-12}^{-5} \sqrt{\left(-\dfrac{4}{t^2}\right)^2+\left(\dfrac{1}{t}\right)^2}\,dt
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int_{-12}^{-5} \sqrt{\dfrac{16}{t^4}+\dfrac{1}{t^2}}\,dt
\end{align}$

In conclusion, this integral gives the arc length of the curve over the interval from $t=-12$ to $t=-5$:

$\displaystyle\int_{-12}^{-5} \sqrt{\dfrac{16}{t^4}+\dfrac{1}{t^2}}\,dt$
